# Research Brief: vala — EV-Maximizing Swindle Engine via Maia-Conditioned Expectimax

*Generated by a multi-agent research workflow (19 agents, 45 findings, 10 deep
reads, 2 adversarially-verified claims) on 2026-05-31. Lightly edited.*

## 1. Does this idea have precedent?
Yes, on two fronts, and we are not the first to try it in chess.

- **Poker (the algorithmic ancestor).** Billings et al.'s **Vexbot** replaced
  minimax with **miximax/miximix** expectimax search, treating the opponent's
  choices as chance nodes weighted by a learned opponent model and playing the
  max-EV action. This is *exactly* vala's architecture with Maia substituted for
  the frequentist hand-history model.
  [Schauenberg/Billings, U. Alberta](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Opponent-Modelling-and-Search-in-Poker-Schauenberg/ec5e950fe6a55bbec57cccf0d272245ae07814b9);
  [Billings PhD thesis](https://poker.cs.ualberta.ca/publications/billings.phd.pdf).
- **Chess (the closest existing build).** Thomas Ahle's
  [chess-openings-expectimax](https://github.com/thomasahle/chess-openings-expectimax)
  runs expectimax over opening trees, opponent replies = empirical human-move
  frequencies (~2M Lichess games), leaves = Stockfish WDL win-expectation. It
  found anti-human lines (e.g., Italian Ng5) that "may not be sound against a
  perfect opponent" — the same swindle tradeoff. vala's two improvements:
  rating-conditioned Maia instead of an unfiltered blitz-dominated distribution,
  and a downside cap.

The **Maia** model itself
([McIlroy-Young et al., KDD 2020](https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~ashton/pubs/maia-kdd2020.pdf))
was explicitly framed for *teaching*, not adversarial exploitation — so the
exploitation engine is a genuinely un-taken direction.

## 2. The right formalism
**Miximax** (expectimax / best-response-to-opponent-model). Engine nodes are
MAX; opponent nodes are **chance nodes** weighted by the model's reply
distribution; you back up expected value, not worst-case value.

```
EV(m) = Σ_r P_Maia(r | s', elo) · (−cp_opp(r)) + (1 − covered) · sound_cp
```

The `(1−covered)·sound_cp` term (unmodeled tail plays soundly) is a sensible
conservatism. The principled tuning knob is the **Restricted Nash Response**
dial ([Johanson et al., NIPS 2007](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1603.03491)): p=0 is
minimax/GTO (safe), p=1 is full best-response (max exploitation, max
exploitability). vala's `margin_cp` and `risk_cp` *are* this dial — lower
`risk_cp` ⇒ closer to minimax ⇒ more robust, less swindle.

## 3. Which Maia & Elo conditioning
Maia-3 (Chessformer, CSSLab) is appropriate — at least as capable as the
published ~50%+ top-1 figures. Maia matches the human move **>50%** of the time
at its target band (~52% peak); worst-case cross-rating (Maia-1900 on 1100s) is
~46% ([KDD 2020](https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~ashton/pubs/maia-kdd2020.pdf)).
Maia-2 added ~2pts and far better skill monotonicity (27% vs 1%), which matters
because vala conditions on opponent Elo.

**Caveat that bites the core thesis:** top-1 accuracy is *not* tail calibration.
The EV formula multiplies `P_Maia` directly as expectations, but swindle EV is
driven by mass on replies 2–6 (the blunder you're baiting) — precisely the
regime where ~50% top-1 says least. Maia treats blunder prediction as a separate,
harder problem. **Verify the Elo convention:** at a human-to-move node,
`elo_self` = the human's rating is correct; `elo_oppo` is vala (no natural Maia
rating — we pass the human's, a neutral default; tune later).

## 4. Top 3 failure modes + mitigations
1. **Best-response is provably unsafe when the model is wrong.** A full
   best-response "is highly exploitable in the worst case"
   ([Ganzfried & Sandholm, Safe Opponent Exploitation](https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~sandholm/safeExploitation.teac15.pdf));
   you cannot deviate to exploit *and* guarantee game value. *Mitigation:* the
   `risk_cp` cap bounds the per-move gift, but within budget it can hand back
   ~1 pawn; against a systematically-wrong model that compounds. Tune
   `risk_cp`/`margin_cp` as the safety dial.
2. **Phantom blunders from imitation-model artifacts.** Unregularized
   best-response to a behavioral-cloning policy "exploits undefined behaviors of
   the BC policy" — brittle moves chasing prediction errors, worst in sharp/rare
   positions where Maia's accuracy is lowest
   ([Hu et al.](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.05125)). *Mitigation:* regularize
   toward the sound move; add an **entropy/confidence gate** — only bait when
   Maia's reply distribution is concentrated.
3. **Cold start / sparse signal.** Vexbot was "highly volatile" early. vala has
   a generic per-position rather than per-player prior. *Mitigation:* the
   per-player [KDD 2022 follow-up](https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~ashton/pubs/maia-individual-kdd2022.pdf)
   sharpens the model once game history exists.

## 5. Metrics to measure
- **Tail calibration:** log-loss/Brier and per-rank hit-rate of replies 2–6 on
  held-out human games at the target Elo. This, not top-1, governs EV validity.
- **The backfire experiment (highest priority):** EV-select vs engine-best
  minimax against opponents that **deviate** from Maia (different-Elo Maia, a
  real engine, randomized replies). Measure objective eval/score conceded when
  the model is wrong.
- **Realized vs predicted blunder rate:** fraction of baits where the human
  actually plays the modeled mistake; if << Maia's P, the tail is miscalibrated.
- **Net score vs a sound baseline**, stratified by opponent Elo — Vexbot's
  lesson: big wins vs weak/predictable players, marginal-to-negative vs strong
  ones. Target the fallible regime explicitly.
